title: Probing topological phase transitions via nonlinear Hall response in strained moiré dice lattice
authors: Gourab Paul, Srijata Lahiri, Bilal Tanatar, Saurabh Basu
abstract: Valley polarized twisted bilayer dice lattice hosts topologically nontrivial flat bands far from charge neutrality due to broken time reversal symmetry, whereas the ones in the vicinity of it remain topologically trivial. However, when both valleys are taken into consideration, the time reversal symmetry is preserved, which poses a serious hindrance to enumerate the valley specific topological phases that rely on the detection of the Berry curvature. In this work, we demonstrate that such a twisted structure with an applied uniaxial strain exhibits a nonlinear Hall effect far from charge neutrality. We ascertain that the nonlinear anomalous Hall signals can serve as a probe for topological phase transitions associated with a specific energy state that is constrained to reside at the lower edge of the middle subband and controlled via a staggered mass. Specifically, we show that the nonlinear anomalous Hall response undergoes a sign reversal across the topological phase boundaries. By tuning the carrier density, we compute the nonlinear Hall response obtained from the Berry curvature dipole, both in the chiral limit, and also when the chiral symmetry is broken. It is further seen that the nonlinear Hall effect is significantly enhanced in the broken chiral symmetry regime.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the nonlinear Hall effect can be used to detect topological phase transitions in a twisted bilayer dice lattice where traditional Hall measurements fail due to time-reversal symmetry. By applying uniaxial strain, the researchers show that the Berry curvature dipole undergoes a sign reversal at phase boundaries, providing a clear experimental signature for band inversion.
main_problem: Detecting and enumerating valley-specific topological phases in systems where time-reversal symmetry is preserved, which renders the conventional linear Hall effect ineffective.
main_result: The nonlinear Hall effect (NLHE) serves as an effective probe for topological phase transitions, exhibiting a characteristic sign reversal across phase boundaries and significant enhancement in the broken chiral symmetry regime.
method: Numerical simulation using a continuum model based on the Bistritzer-MacDonald formalism to compute Berry curvature density, Chern numbers, and the Berry curvature dipole (BCD).
model_or_system: A twisted bilayer dice lattice (TBDL) under applied uniaxial strain, modeled via a continuum Hamiltonian that includes interlayer hopping and broken symmetries (C3 and inversion).
key_observables: Nonlinear Hall response (NLHE), Berry curvature dipole (BCD) components (Dx, Dy), Chern number, and Berry curvature density.
important_parameters: Twist angle (theta), uniaxial strain (epsilon), staggered/onsite mass (Delta), carrier density (mu), and chiral symmetry regimes.
main_assumptions: Neglect of spin-orbit coupling and specific assumptions regarding interlayer hopping hierarchy.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of dice lattice and moire Brillouin zone; Fig 2: Band structures under strain; Fig 3: Chern number phase diagram; Fig 4: Berry curvature density changes across phase transitions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of detecting topology in TRS systems, describes the strained TBDL model and symmetry breaking, presents numerical results for band structures and Chern phases, and demonstrates the NLHE as a diagnostic tool via BCD analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram of the monolayer dice lat- tice. (a) The blue, red, and green dots represent the A, B, and C sublattice atoms, respectively. The primitive lattice vectors are denoted by a1 and a2. The NN hopping am- plitude between A-B and B-C sublattice atoms is denoted by t, while the NNN hopping amplitude between A-A, B-B, and C-C subalattice sites is represented by t1. (b) Brillouin zones of the top and bottom layers of the TBDL, shown by blue and red dashed lines, respectively, rotated by a rela- tive twist angle θ with respect to each other. The corre- sponding moiré Brillouin zone (MBZ) is characterized by the

FIG. 2. Band structures of TBDL at the magic angle in presence of a uniaxial strain Ep = 0.2%. Panel (a) presents the energy spectrum in the chiral limit of the system, while panel (b) shows the corresponding energy dispersions in the broken chiral symmetry regime (implemented by setting w1 = w2 = 60 meV), for the lowest band at the middle band edge and its nearest high energy band. The calculations are performed with ∆= 170 meV for panel (a) and ∆= 60 meV for panel (b). The energy dispersions along the three distinct C3 symmetric paths (Γ–M1, Γ–M2, and Γ–M3), represented by solid, dashed, and dotted blue lines, deviate from one another, indicating the breaking of C3 symmetry in the system....

FIG. 3. Chern number phase diagram for the lowest eigenstate of the middle sub-band. The figure shows emergent topological phases for the band index n = 162 of strained TBDL, as a function of the onsite mass ∆and the twist angle θ. The system has been strained weakly with the magnitude being E = 0.2%.

FIG. 4. Changes in Berry curvature density across phase transition lines. The change in Berry curvature density across transitions lines from (a-i) Phase (I) →(a-ii) Phase (II), (b-i) Phase (I) →(b-ii) Phase (IV), (c-i) Phase (II) →(c-ii) Phase (III) and (d-i) Phase (III) →(d-ii) Phase (IV), corresponding to Fig. 3, has been shown. The analysis has been constrained mainly to the bands with index n = 161, 162 and 163, which reside at the edge of the middle sub-band. The reversal in the sign of Berry curvature density (across the vertical panels) at the band anti-crossing points correspond to the occurrence of band inversion due to gap closing. The system parameters for the different phases...

FIG. 5. Reversal in the sign of BCD across topological phases. The rectangular components of BCD, namely Dx and Dy, flip their sign on transitioning from (a) Phase (I) →Phase (II), (b) Phase (I) →Phase (IV), (c) Phase (II) →Phase (III) and (d) Phase (III) →Phase (IV), as also represented in Fig. 4, thus making it an efficient probe to detect topological phase transitions in time reversal symmetric systems.
